one_line_takeaway: EroSeg is a white-box segmentation attack that targets low-confidence pixels first and progressively propagates perturbations to higher-confidence ones, and using it as the adversarial-training attack yields more robust DeepLabV3/PSPNet models (higher mIoU under CW/PGD10/PGD20) than training with PGD, SegPGD, CosPGD, or RP-PGD.
key_finding: As a pure attack, EroSeg drives mIoU below 3% by the 10th iteration and below 5% by the 7th iteration on PASCAL VOC, outperforming PGD, CosPGD, SegPGD, and RP-PGD at matched iteration counts (Fig. 5). As an adversarial-training generator (Table 1), EroSeg3-AT and EroSeg7-AT achieve the highest robust mIoU against CW, PGD10, and PGD20 in nearly every model/dataset combination compared to PGD-AT, SegPGD-AT, CosPGD-AT, and RP-PGD-AT at the same iteration counts; averaged across the reported settings the paper states EroSeg3-AT and EroSeg7-AT reach mean mIoUs of 30.86% and 34.00% respectively, versus 28.65% and 31.53% for the best existing methods at matched iteration counts. Ablations show performance stabilizes at tau_0=0.8, beta=0.8, and lambda=0.7, which were selected as the operating hyperparameters.
core_contribution: The paper proposes EroSeg, a white-box adversarial attack for semantic segmentation models, and EroSeg-AT, an adversarial training (AT) framework that uses EroSeg-generated examples to harden segmentation models. EroSeg has two components: (1) Confidence-based Selection of Sensitive Pixels, which identifies a set of 'sensitive' pixels S0 = {(i,j) | c_i,j < tau_0 and correctly classified}, where c_i,j is the pixel's max softmax confidence and tau_0=0.8 is an initial threshold; and (2) Progressive Perturbation Propagation, which grows the confidence threshold over attack iterations via tau_t = 1 - (1-tau_0)*e^(-beta*t) so perturbation targeting progressively spreads from low-confidence (vulnerable) pixels to higher-confidence (stable) pixels, plus a class-aware weighting term (weight lambda for foreground vs 1-lambda for background) in the masked cross-entropy loss used to craft perturbations. The authors motivate the design with two empirical observations (Fig. 2 in the paper): attacking only pixels with confidence exactly 1 is nearly ineffective, and attacking foreground pixels is more effective than attacking all pixels uniformly ('perturbation smoothing' from contextual/neighboring-pixel correction is identified as the reason existing attacks under-perform).
threat_model: White-box adversarial attack against semantic segmentation models (pixel-wise dense classifiers). Attacker has full access to model gradients and outputs; perturbation delta is constrained by an l_p norm bound (epsilon = 8/255 in experiments, following prior work SegPGD/RP-PGD/CosPGD). The attack is used both offensively (to evaluate/degrade segmentation model accuracy, measured via mIoU) and defensively as the inner-maximization attack inside a min-max adversarial training objective (Eq. 1) to produce more robust segmentation models. No black-box, physical-world, or transfer-attack setting is evaluated.
limitations: The authors explicitly state in the Limitation section that EroSeg has not been extended to detection tasks (only semantic segmentation) due to structural differences between segmentation and detection models, and identify this as a direction for future work. Beyond the authors' own statement: the study is confined to two CNN architectures (DeepLabV3, PSPNet) with a single backbone (ResNet50) and two datasets; no transformer-based segmentation models are tested. All evaluation is white-box with a single fixed perturbation budget (8/255); no black-box or transfer-attack results are reported. The paper does not report inference/attack runtime or computational overhead of the progressive propagation scheme relative to baselines.

quantitative_results: Pure attack: EroSeg reduces mIoU below 3% by the 10th attack iteration and below 5% by the 7th iteration on PASCAL VOC, outperforming PGD/CosPGD/SegPGD/RP-PGD at matched iterations
AT comparison (Table 1), DeepLabV3/PASCAL VOC, 3-iteration attacks used for AT: EroSeg3-AT robust mIoU under CW/PGD10/PGD20 = 52.61/34.58/21.62 vs RP-PGD3-AT (best baseline) 51.80/33.28/21.06
AT comparison (Table 1), PSPNet/PASCAL VOC, 3-iteration: EroSeg3-AT = 38.78/26.24/18.54 vs RP-PGD3-AT = 37.31/25.68/17.64
AT comparison (Table 1), DeepLabV3/PASCAL VOC, 7-iteration: EroSeg7-AT = 54.62/39.88/26.59 vs RP-PGD7-AT (best baseline) 52.77/39.06/26.01
Averaged across reported settings: EroSeg3-AT mean mIoU = 30.86% vs 28.65% for best existing 3-iteration AT methods; EroSeg7-AT mean mIoU = 34.00% vs 31.53% for best existing 7-iteration AT methods
Ablation: performance stabilizes at initial threshold tau_0=0.8; growth factor beta=0.8; foreground weight lambda=0.7 gives best performance
baselines_compared: DDCAT (cited as earliest AT method for segmentation, not directly benchmarked in Table 1)
